rulings.Case Title: MARK MIGDAL et al VS. MASHA LEVINSON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions, Evidentiary Sacntions And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 12, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF MARK MIGDAL, MARK MIGDAL AS TRUSTEE OF THE MARK MIGDAL 2000 Motion For Terminating Sanctions, Evidentiary Sanctions And Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Kathy Gallo, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is unopposed, as the court has no courtesy copies. Plaintiff's request for monetary sanctions pursuant to C.C.P. Sections 22030.030, 2030.300(e) and 2031.310(i) in the amount of $3060.00 is granted against defendants and their attorneys of record. Sanctions to be made payable to plaintiff and their attorneys of Record Cooper, White & Cooper no later than December 1, 2014. Plaintiff's request that the Court impose a sanction pursuant to C.C.P. Section 177.5 for violation of the Court's for violation of the Court's September 30, 2014 order is granted. Defendants and their attorneys of record are to pay the Clerk of the Superior Court for the City and County of San Francisco $1500 by December 1, 2014. Plaintiff's request for terminating sanctions is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to klgallo@discoveryreferee.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GRANT & WEBER, A CORPORATION VS. GREGORY R. OLBRIGHT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 12, 2014, Line 6, CROSS DEFENDANT CENTRE FOR NEURO SKILLS, A CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. The demurrer to the second cause of action for breach of the implied covenant is sustained without leave to amend. Cross-complainant can only recover contractual damages on this claim and can seek the same relief in the first cause of action for breach of contract. The demurrer to the third cause of action for Unfair Business Practices is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Cross-complainant should amend the claim to (1) clarify that he is seeking relief under the UCL (Business and Professions Code sec. 17200) rather than the UPA; and (2) allege whether cross-defendant is being sued for an unfair, unlawful, and/or fraudulent business practice. Cross-defendant's argument regarding whether Health and Safety Code sec. 127420 applies in this case raises a factual issue that cannot be resolved on demurrer. The demurrer to the fourth cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress is sustained with leave to amend to plead a claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress and/or an independent duty arising from tort law. Cross-complainant fails to allege a duty independent of the contract. The demurrer to the fifth cause of action for elder abuse is overruled because defendant raises factual issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: LESLIE FINLEY VS. DREXEL A. BRADSHAW et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compelo Further Response To Defts Req For Production
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 12, 2014, Line 3, DEFENDANT DREXEL BRADSHAW, BRADSHAW AND ASSOCIATES, P.C. Motion To Compel Further Response To Defendants Request For Production. Pro Tem Judge Kathy Gallo, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is denied. The discovery requests became moot and Plaintiff had no obligation to respond to the discovery once the Second Amended complaint was filed and Bradshaw P.C. was no longer a party. However, Bradshaw P.C. can reserve the same discovery if it becomes a party to the action at a later date. All requests for sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to klgallo@discoveryreferee.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 12, 2014, Line 10, PLAINTIFF CHRISTOPHER HECTOR Motion For Terminating Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Kathy Gallo, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. The court also finds that the moving papers failed to show a history of discovery abuse that would warrant terminating sanctions.
